coredata.dc:description: Recently, energy and environmental crises have aggravated sharply, which results in the eco-friendly and high-efficiency polyethylene glycol (PEG) lubricating oils getting increasingly attention in lubrication. Advancing in PEG-based lubricating oils, especially their high-performance nano-additives is vitally important for energy conservation and reducing pollution. Therefore, a kind of surface and core dual-designed carbon dots (Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N) modified by ionic liquid groups ([AMIm][N(CF3SO2)2]) and doped by polymetallic atoms (Zn, Cu, and Fe) were first synthesized and creatively used as the nano-additives for the PEG200 base oil. Adding 1.0 wt% of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N can endow the PEG200 base oil with optimal friction-reduction, anti-wear, and load-supporting performances, the maximal increase amplitudes of approximately 46.6 %, 66.3 %, and 125.0 %, respectively. Additionally, the tribological properties of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N are without any weakening even under high load and long-time operating conditions, exhibiting good practicability and application prospects. Theoretical and experimental studies have illustrated the excellent tribological behaviors of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N assigned to the synergy of film-forming and nano-lubrication effects.

head.ce:title: Surface and core dual-designed carbon dots toward high-efficiency nano-lubricant additives for polyethylene glycol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, energy and environmental crises have aggravated sharply, which results in the eco-friendly and high-efficiency polyethylene glycol (PEG) lubricating oils getting increasingly attention in lubrication. Advancing in PEG-based lubricating oils, especially their high-performance nano-additives is vitally important for energy conservation and reducing pollution. Therefore, a kind of surface and core dual-designed carbon dots (Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N) modified by ionic liquid groups ([AMIm][N(CF3SO2)2]) and doped by polymetallic atoms (Zn, Cu, and Fe) were first synthesized and creatively used as the nano-additives for the PEG200 base oil. Adding 1.0 wt% of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N can endow the PEG200 base oil with optimal friction-reduction, anti-wear, and load-supporting performances, the maximal increase amplitudes of approximately 46.6 %, 66.3 %, and 125.0 %, respectively. Additionally, the tribological properties of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N are without any weakening even under high load and long-time operating conditions, exhibiting good practicability and application prospects. Theoretical and experimental studies have illustrated the excellent tribological behaviors of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N assigned to the synergy of film-forming and nano-lubrication effects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With fossil energy such as coal, petroleum, and natural gas increasingly drying up and environmental pollution gradually worsening, energy conservation and emission reduction have already become a priority all over the world [1]. Research shows that a large quantity of the world's energy is consumed to overcome friction, which causes an economic toll that reaches up to hundreds of billions of dollars each year [2,3]. Plenty of evidence has proved that induced lubricating oils into the mechanical components could memorably reduce their friction and wear, significantly promoting energy efficiency, reducing greenhouse gas emissions, and decreasing mechanical failures [4,5]. Therefore, lubricating oils play an essential role in industrial production and daily life, particularly in alleviating energy crisis and environmental pollution [6]. Although the commonly used mineral oils and synthetic oils possess outstanding lubricity, all of them are difficult to degrade in the natural environment [7–9]. Additionally, the harmless disposal of waste lubricating oils is not only expensive and has high energy consumption but also will produce associated pollutants, causing the application of conventional mineral oils and synthetic oils to be increasingly limited [10,11]. In this case, eco-friendly PEG-based lubricating oils are getting increasingly attention because of their outstanding lubricity, viscosity-temperature characteristics, and degradability [12–16]. Despite PEG being widely applied in various industries owing to its good physicochemical properties, the application of PEG in lubrication is in its infancy [17–22]. Meanwhile, the inherent defects including poor oxidation, high-temperature, and shear resistances as well as weak friction-reduction and anti-wear performances cause PEG-based lubricating oils easily to decompose under relatively harsh working conditions and ultimately lose their lubrication behavior [23,24]. With the working conditions of modern mechanical equipment increasingly demanding, the above defects greatly limited the practical application of PEG-based lubricating oils [25].

It is generally accepted that lubricant additives are the essence of high-performance lubricating oils. Therefore, adding low doses of the appropriate lubricant additives can efficaciously solve the before-mentioned imperfections of PEG-based lubricating oils, promoting their lubricity, applied range, and utility value [26–28]. Many studies have suggested that nano-lubricant additives relying on high chemical inertness, outstanding tribological behavior, abundant friction-reduction and anti-wear modes, great extreme pressure resistance, and eco-friendliness have become the research hotspot in the domain of PEG-based lubricating oils [29–33]. Among them, CDs-based additives are becoming one of the most distinguished representatives in nano-lubricant additives. As is well-known, CDs are a new type of carbon-based nano-material (diameter<10 nm), was fortuitously discovered in 2004 [34] and purposively synthesized in 2006 [35], has received considerable attention in the field of nano-lubricant additive since 2015 which was first used as a lubricant additive [36]. Numerous studies have shown that CDs as additives can effectually promote the tribological performance of PEG-based lubricating oils because of their good physicochemical property and multiple friction-reduction and anti-wear modes [37–40]. First, CDs-based additives depending on their super-small particle size could rapidly enter the frictional interfaces to form the physical adsorption film by van der Waals' force or electrostatic interaction, preventing the rubbing surfaces from direct contact [41]. Furthermore, the entered CDs can also play the nano-lubrication effect (rolling, mending, and polishing effects) to reduce the roughness and contact pressure of rubbing surfaces, further decreasing the friction and wear of the friction pairs [42,43]. Then, as the tribo-test goes on, CDs-based additives will gradually react with the rubbing surfaces to establish a firm tribochemical reaction film (tribo-film) to replace the physical adsorption film, efficiently improving the friction-reduction and anti-wear performances of PEG-based lubricating oils [44,45]. However, the commonly used CDs-based additives are mainly conventional organic molecules functionalized CDs, whose tribological behaviors still have much room for improvement [46]. Therefore, considerable efforts have been devoted to developing new ways for further enhancing the tribological properties of CDs-based additives.

Surface functionalization is considered an effective strategy to achieve the above goal, which is aimed at covalently grafting the functionalized groups (including small organic molecules, polymers, ionic liquids, and so on) on the surface of CDs-based additives to improve their dispersion embedding stability in PEG-based lubricating oils and embedding stability on the rubbing surfaces [47,48]. Meanwhile, the functional groups can accelerate CDs-based additives to build the physical adsorption film on the rubbing surfaces by van der Waals' force or electrostatic interaction and react with rubbing surfaces to form a stronger tribo-film rapidly [49,50]. Additionally, studies have suggested that doping CDs-based additives with metal atoms (such as Ag, Ni, Cu, Zn, etc.) is also a crucial channel to improve their tribological behaviors further [51]. The doped metal atoms can accelerate the tribochemical reaction between the CDs-based additives and rubbing surfaces, thus rapidly forming the tribo-film [52–54]. Currently, the related research only involved the monmetallic atom-doped CDs-based additives. Although the metal atoms doped CDs-based additives showed excellent tribological behaviors, the relevant research is still in its infancy and little is known about the tribological behaviors of the polymetallic atoms co-doped CDs-based additives. In consideration of the enormous potential of the introduction of ionic liquid groups and the doping of metal atoms, especially polymetallic atoms co-doping in improving the tribological behaviors of CDs-based additives, thereby combining the above approaches were expected to further promote their tribological behaviors signally, while no relevant studies have been reported. As a consequence, exploiting newfashioned polymetallic atoms co-doped and ionic liquids functionalized CDs-based additives with prominent friction-reduction and anti-wear behaviors will open a new avenue for the CDs-based additives.

In this work, the new-type Zn, Cu, and Fe co-doped CDs (Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N) with covalently modifying by ionic liquid groups were creatively synthesized by pyrolysis and anion exchange procedures. What is noteworthy is that the synthetic process uninvolved any purification procedure, showing good universality. The geometrical morphology, crystal structure, elemental composition, and thermal stability of the synthesized Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N were scientifically studied by diversiform microscopic, spectroscopic, and thermal analysis techniques. The involved characterization techniques were described in the supplementary material (SM). Then, Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N were directly used as the lubricant additives for the PEG200 base oil without any auxiliary dispersant. The tribological behaviors of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 lubrication systems were comprehensively evaluated by a representative UMT-3 tribometer. The influences of the additive concentration, load, and duration on the friction-reduction and anti-wear functions of the lubrication systems were deeply revealed. In addition, the cogent lubrication mechanism of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N as additives was put forward by employing various surface analysis techniques. The descriptions of wear track surface analyses were also provided in the SM.

Citric acid monohydrate (95.0 %, CA) and 1-aminopropyl-3-methyl-imidazolium bromide (99.0 %, [AMIm][Br]) were purchased from Tianjin Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China) and Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics (China), respectively. Zinc, copper, and ferrous gluconates (98.0 %) were gained from Beijing Macklin Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). Ethyl alcohol (99.7 %) and bis(trifluoromethane)sulfonimide lithium (LiN(CF3SO2)2, 98.5 %) were obtained from Shanghai Aladin Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). Polyethylene glycol (molecular weight of approximately 200, PEG200) was gained from Energy Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). All the chemicals and reagents were directly used in this work as received without further purification. Additionally, deionized water was used throughout the experiment from an OSJ-E-UP ultrapure water purifier (Oubolai, China).

The polymetallic atoms co-doped CDs were synthesized by a typical down-to-up strategy, i.e., direct pyrolysis method combined with the anion-exchange approach, the corresponding synthetic procedures are shown in Fig. 1 . First, CA (0.03 mol), zinc gluconate (0.005 mol), copper gluconate (0.005 mol), and ferrous gluconate (0.005 mol) were sequentially added into a three-neck flask (50 mL), which coupled with a reflux condenser. Subsequently, the three-neck flask was transferred to an oil-bath pan and heated at 200 °C under magnetic stirring and an N2 atmosphere. When all the chemicals in the flask were melted completely, [AMIm][Br] (0.03 mol) was rapidly added into the flask and then kept at 200 °C for 3 h. When the flask was cooled to the indoor temperature, the obtained water-dispersible CDs. Then, 30 mL of deionized water was poured into the above flask under vigorous stirring, till the above CDs were absolutely dispersed into the deionized water, gaining the black dispersion. Soon afterward, a certain quality of LiN(CF3SO2)2 was tardily added to the dispersion to trigger the anion-exchange reaction under vigorous stirring until the dispersion gradually became colorless and transparent. In this process, the Br‾ on the surfaces of CDs were gradually substituted by N(CF3SO2)2‾, and the above CDs from water dispersibility transformed into non-aqueous dispersibility, resulting in they would separate out from the dispersion, thereby acquiring precipitates. After drying at 60 °C for 48 h, the gained light black gel was the Zn, Cu, and Fe co-doped CDs with grafting by the ionic liquid of [AMIm][N(CF3SO2)2], abbreviating as Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N.

A series of different concentrations of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersions were prepared by a conventional dilution method. Briefly, the prepared Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N was quantificationally dispersed into a given weight of the PEG200 base oil under ultrasonic treatment for 30 min to obtain the high weight concentration of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion (2.0 wt%). Various concentrations of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersions (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25, and 1.5 wt%) were gained by utilizing the PEG200 base oil to dilute the 2.0 wt% of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion under ultrasonic treatment.

The lubrication behaviors of the Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersions were evaluated via a typical ball-on-plate type tribometer (UMT-3 tribometer, Bruker) with a linear reciprocating mode under steel-steel contacts. Specifically, a commercially available AISI-52100 steel ball (12.7 mm in diameter and hardness of 59–61 HRC) slid reciprocally against a low-stationary AISI-52100 steel plate (50 mm × 40 mm × 3 mm in size and 59–61 HRC in hardness). The steel plate was mounted in a holder and connected through a lever coupled with a friction force transducer. The friction coefficients (FC) were automatically recorded using a computer coupled with a high-sensitivity sensor. To obtain convincing average friction coefficient (AFC) and average wear volume (AWV) values with the error bars, each test was conducted thrice under the following conditions: load, 50–900 N; duration, 20 min; frequency, 5 Hz; amplitude, 5 mm; ambient temperature and humidity. The AFC and AWV values were calculated as the average of all data points on the corresponding FC curve and the average of the WV values of three tribo-tests. The error bars were calculated as the standard error of the means.

Initially, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was adopted to investigate the geometrical morphology of the synthesized Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N. In Fig. 2 (a), the corresponding TEM image indicates that Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N possess a relatively ideal quasi-spheroidal shape and uniform diameter distribution. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) characterization result shows the average diameter of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N is about 6.86 nm (the inset in Fig. 2(a)). The high-resolution TEM images in Fig. 2(b) display that Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N present a monodisperse state in the dispersion medium. In addition, there are no noticeable lattice fringe can be found in Fig. 2(b), intuitively elucidating that Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N predominantly consist of amorphous structures. This finding can be further certified by the result of X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization in Fig. 2(c). The XRD pattern of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N exhibits a wide and short peak located at 20.1°, which belonged to the (002) plane of carbon-based materials, adequately confirming that Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N were basically made up of amorphous structures [55,56].

Subsequently, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was implemented to reveal the surface functional group of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N. In Fig. 2(d), the peaks centered at 3476, 2931, 1641−1622, 1462, 1350, 1191−1057, and 850−610 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibrations of –OH, asymmetric stretching vibration of C–H in –CH2−, deformation vibration of N–H in amido bonds, stretching vibration of C–H in –HCCH−, stretching vibration C–N, asymmetric stretching vibration of C–O–C in ester groups, and deformation vibration of C–H in –(CH2)n−, respectively [17,57,58]. Meanwhile, the majority of characteristic peaks of the [AMIm][N(CF3SO2)2] (Fig. S1) can be also observed in the FTIR spectrum of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N. These findings demonstrate that many [AMIm][N(CF3SO2)2] groups were covalently grafted onto the surfaces of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N. As illustrated in Fig. 2(e), the TGA curve of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N shows that the residual mass of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N is approximately 37.1 % when the thermal decomposition temperature reaches 800 °C under an N2 atmosphere. Certainly, the remnants are the undecomposed rigid carbon cores of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N. Meanwhile, two obvious decomposition stages appeared in the TGA curve. Specifically, the weight loss at 100−320 °C assigned to the evaporation of the absorbed water and decomposition of oxygen-containing groups, and the weight loss at 320−460 °C primarily arose from the decomposition of the high thermal stability of [AMIm][N(CF3SO2)2] groups.

Finally, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted to analyze the element compositions and bonding states of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N deeply. As shown in Fig. 2(f) and Table 1 , the XPS characterization results suggest that Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N are mainly composed of C, O, F, N, S, Zn, Cu, and Fe. The corresponding composition formula by calculating can be expressed as C3.4O2.03F0.57N0.23S0.11Fe0.05Zn0.05Cu0.04. In Fig. 3 (a), the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N can be resolved into seven peaks at 293.9, 292.8, 289.3, 288.5, 286.6, 285.1, and 284.5 eV, respectively ascribing to the bonds of –CF3, π−π* satellite, C–S, CO/CN, C–O/C–N, C–C, and CC [6,10]. Meanwhile, the fitting peaks of C–O (533.0 eV for O1s), CO (531.5 eV for O1s), C–N (400.2 eV for N1s), CN (399.4 eV for N1s), C–S (170.0 eV for S2p), sulfate (169.0 eV for S2p), C–F (689.1 eV for F1s), and trifluoride (688.5 eV for F1s) were also detected in Fig. 3(a−c) [59,60]. In addition to the above-mentioned peaks, two new fitting peaks of SO at 534.1 eV (O1s) and S–N at 401.8 eV (N1s) were also found in Fig. 3(a) and (b), respectively [61]. Exhilaratingly, the bonds of Zn–O (1022.2 eV for Zn2p in Fig. 3(c)), Cu(ΙΙ) (933.3 eV for Cu2p in Fig. 3(d)), as well as Fe–O (712.5 eV for Fe2p in Fig. 3(d)) emerged in the high-resolution XPS spectra [53,62–64]. The above results not only jointly revealed that the Zn, Cu, and Fe atoms were successfully doped into Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N, but also further certified that the lots of [AMIm][N(CF3SO2)2] groups were covalently attached onto the surfaces of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion stability of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N in PEG200 base oil
ce:section.ce:para: Proverbially, the dispersion stability of a fire-new nano-based lubricant additive in the corresponding base oil is often a determining factor in whether it can be applied. As a result, the convenient still-setting and DLS methods were employed to systematically evaluate the dispersion stability of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N in the PEG200 base oil. By comparing to Fig. 4 (a) and (c), no apparent agglomerates and sediments were found in the containers whether the 1.0 wt% of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion after preparation or was stored for three months. In a macroscopic view, this result suggested that the prepared Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N possess good dispersion stability in the PEG200 base oil. Fig. 4(b) shows that the average diameter of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N in the dispersion is approximately 7.03 nm when it is just being prepared. It is noteworthy that the average diameter of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N in the dispersion only slightly increased, compared to the original Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N. The increment in average diameter is ascribed to many PEG200 molecules that were adsorbed onto the surfaces of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N by intermolecular forces. Unquestionably, the adsorption sites on the surfaces of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N are relatively limited, once the adsorption capacity reaches saturation, the average diameter of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N would not be increased. Hence, after three months of standing, the average diameters of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N in the dispersion only increased from 7.03 to 7.33 nm, with an increased amplitude of only 4.27 %. On a micro-level, these results revealed that Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N in the lubrication system did not emerge agglomeration, indirectly reflecting their outstanding dispersion stability in the PEG200 base oil.

As is generally accepted, the tribological behaviors of the nano-based lubricant additives in lubrication systems are closely associated with their added concentration. In consequence, the effect of the added concentration of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N on the lubrication performance of the PEG200 base oil was investigated preferentially. Noticeably, the friction coefficient (FC) curve of the PEG200 base oil in Fig. 5 (a) is apparently higher than that of various concentrations of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersions. Among them, the 1.0 wt% of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion displays the lowest FC curve, meaning that the most suitable added concentration for Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N may be about 1.0 wt%. Generally, lots of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N in the dispersion will be consumed to establish the lubrication film during the tribo-test. When the added concentration of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N is lower than 1.0 wt%, there is not enough Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N to form the sufficient thickness and strength lubrication film for efficiently reducing the friction and wear of the friction pairs, causing the lubrication performance of the dispersion is limited. On the contrary, the abrasive wear caused by the aggregation of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N will occur at the high added concentration, hence weakening the tribological performance of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N. Therefore, the existence of the most suitable added concentration is a common phenomenon for nano-lubricant additives [65,66]. As illustrated in Fig. 5(b), the average friction coefficient (AFC) and average wear volume (AWV) of the various Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersions first declined and then increased with increasing the added concentration of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N. When the added concentration of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N is approximately 1.0 wt%, both the AFC and AWV reach their minimums, confirming that 1.0 wt% is the optimal added concentration. Specifically, the AFC and AWV of the Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion (1.0 wt%) reduced by 46.6 % and 66.3 %, respectively, compared to the PEG200 base oil.

As shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d), adding 1.0 wt% of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N, the load-supporting capacity of the PEG200 base oil can be increased from 400 to 900 N, increasing by 125.0 %. The above fascinating findings elucidated that Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N as additives could markedly promote the friction-reduction, anti-wear, and load-supporting behaviors of the PEG200 base oil, exhibiting great application prospects. Generally, the long-time operating performance of nano-lubricant additives under relatively harsh working conditions was deemed to be a key factor in deciding their practical value. In this case, to assess the tribological behaviors of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N as additives under relatively harsh working conditions, the tribo-tests were performed by prolonging the frictional duration from 20 to 120 min and increasing the load from 50 to 100 N. Fig. 5(e) shows the FC curve of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion (1.0 wt%) is much lower and smother than that of the PEG200 base oil under identical test conditions. In Fig. 5(f), in comparison to the PEG200 base oil, the AFC and AWV of the Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion declined by 46.3 % and 62.8 %. It was evident that the friction-reduction and anti-wear performances of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N in the lubrication system did not appear weakening phenomenon even though under high load and long-time operating conditions. According to the above findings, it can thus be concluded that Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N played an irreplaceable role in promoting the friction-reduction and anti-wear behaviors of the lubrication system.

To gain insight into the mode of action of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N on the rubbing surfaces, the morphology, geometric dimensioning, elemental composition, and film-forming state of the weak tracks were deeply studied by three-dimensional (3D) optical microscope, SEM, EDS, XPS, and TEM characterizations. Fig. 6 gives the two-dimensional (2D) cross-section profile, surface topography, and 3D morphology of the wear tracks lubricated by the PEG200 base oil and Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion (1.0 wt%) under the same tribo-test conditions. In Fig. 6(a), the wear track lubricated by the PEG200 base oil is relatively deep and wide, with a width of approximately 350.42 μm and a depth of about 5.39 μm. Bracingly, the wear track lubricated by Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion is smoother, narrower, and shallower than those of the PEG200 base oil. Adding 1.0 wt% of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N, the corresponding width and depth were reduced to 229.18 and 2.49 μm, and the decreasing amplitudes severally are 34.6 % and 53.8 %. Additionally, a similar situation can be also observed in Fig. 6(c) and (d). Under high load and long-time operating conditions, the size of the wear track lubricated by the PEG200 base oil is still much bigger than that of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion (1.0 wt%). In detail, when Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N were introduced into the PEG200 base oil, which width declined from 410.85 to 290.51 μm and the depth decreased from 10.51 to 5.81 μm, respectively reducing by 29.3 % and 44.7 %.

Many strip-shaped scratches can be observed on the wear track surface lubricated by the PEG200 base oil (Fig. 7 (a)), manifesting that the rubbing surfaces appeared in direct contact during the tribo-test. As shown in Fig. 7(b) and (c), lots of deep pits (marked by the yellow arrows) that stemmed from the serious abrasive wear appeared on the high-resolution SEM images. It has been theoretically predicted that many PEG200 molecules on the rubbing surfaces would decompose under high frictional heat, contact pressure, and shear stress conditions, leading to the metallic rubbing surfaces that emerged in direct contact, thereby generating lots of deep scratches and large-grained abrasive dust. Meanwhile, the abrasive dust on the rubbing surfaces will give rise to grievous abrasive wear, considerably increasing the friction and wear of the friction pairs. In Fig. 7(d), the SEM image shows a large number of white-spotted zones present on the wear track surface lubricated by the Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion (1.0 wt%). In addition, the number and depth of the scratches are significantly reduced and shallowed, respectively. In Fig. 7(e) and (f), there are no deep pits were found on the high-resolution SEM images, the above white spots (marked by the blue arrows) should be the reactants between the Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion and the rubbing surfaces during the tribo-test. The elemental maps (Fig. S2) from the areas of high-resolution SEM images reflect that the wear track surface lubricated by the PEG200 base oil is mainly made up of C, O, and Fe. In addition to these elements, the wear track surface of the Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion also contained a great many of N, F, S, Zn, and Cu, which are the characteristic elements of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N. These findings are highly consistent with the results of XPS characterization in Fig. 8 (a) and Table S1, confirming that Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N in the dispersion have directly participated in reducing the wear of the rubbing surfaces. As shown in Table S1, the elemental composition and content of wear track surfaces on the steel ball and plate are almost the same.

The characteristic fitting peaks of metal oxides, metal carbonates, and organic compounds (composed of C and O) were detected in the high-resolution Fe2p, C1s, and O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 8(a) and (b)) of the wear track surface, which was lubricated by the PEG200 base oil. This result reflects that a portion of PEG200 molecules and their decomposers reacted with the metallic friction surface to produce some new compounds. It is worth noting that the above peaks can be also found in the Fe2p, C1s, and O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 8(c) and (d)) of the wear track surface lubricated by the 1.0 wt% of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion. In addition to the before-mentioned fitting peaks, some new peaks at 293.0, 292.5, and 285.4 eV assigned to the bonds of –CF3, π–π* satellite, and C–C were discovered in the O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 8(c)) [6,67]. Although the peak position may have shifted slightly, the peaks of C–F (689.1 eV) and trifluoride (688.5 eV) also emerged in the F1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 8(d)) [68]. Meanwhile, the typical fitting peaks of S–N (401.8 eV for N1s), C–N (400.5 eV for N1s), CN (399.5 eV for N1s), S–C (170.0 eV for S2p), Zn–O (1022.1 eV for Zn2p), Cu2p1/2 (952.3 eV for Cu2p), as well as Cu(II) oxide (952.3 eV for Cu2p) can be also observed in Fig. 8(e) and (f) [53]. These fitting peaks belong to the typical peaks of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N, suggesting that lots of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N were embedded into the wear track surface. Additionally, the peaks of metal sulfates at 168.8 eV and metal sulfites at 167.2 eV were found in the S2p XPS spectrum. These results indicated that the complicated tribochemical reactions between the metallic rubbing surfaces and the Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion should happen during the tribo-test, generating the metal oxides, metal carbonates, metal sulfates, metal sulfites, and organic compounds (composed of C, O, N, F, and S).

Finally, the bright-field cross-section TEM technique was employed to investigate the film-forming state of the wear track surface lubricated by the 1.0 wt% of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion. Fig. 9 (a) shows an obvious tribo-film with an average thickness of more than 100 nm can be found between the Pt and steel layers, further verifying that the complicated tribochemical reactions on the rubbing surfaces have happened during the tribo-test to establish the tribo-film. Astoundingly, a layer of relatively regular lamella on the bottom of the tribo-film can be intuitively observed. In Fig. 9(b), the selected area and high-resolution TEM (inset) images demonstrate that the above lamella is mainly composed of spheroid nanoparticles with an average diameter of approximately 6.0 nm, which is very close to the Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N (6.89 nm). Combining the results of XPS characterization, the nanoparticles should be Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N. The difference value in average diameter attributed to the some [AMIm][N(CF3SO2)2] groups on the surfaces of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N was consumed for forming the tribo-film during the tribo-test, resulting the their average diameter being decreased lightly. Then, the elemental maps (Fig. 9(c))) and EDS pattern (Fig. S3) of the selected area illustrate the tribo-film mainly composed of Fe, C, O, N, F, S, Cu, and Zn, which results were almost perfectly consistent with XPS and SEM characterizations.

According to the experimental results and previous reports, the compelling mode of action of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N as lubricant additives in the PEG200 base oil was proposed (Fig. 10 ) [69–71]. Proverbially, the metallic friction surfaces are extremely rugged at the micro level, commonly, the metallic friction surfaces are positively charged owing to lots of low-energy electrons emitted from their asperities during the friction process. When the friction pairs were lubricated by the PEG200 base oil, partial PEG200 molecules on the rubbing surfaces were decomposed under high frictional heat and contact pressure as well as shearing action, causing the asperities would occur in direct contact. The direct contact of the rubbing surfaces will lead to the interface temperature and pressure rising rapidly, further accelerating the decomposition of the PEG200 base oil. At the same time, the decomposers and PEG200 molecules reacted with metallic rubbing surfaces to build the thin tribo-film, which is mostly constituted by metal oxides, metal carbonates, and organic compounds (composed of C and O). Although the established tribo-film could transitorily restrain the rubbing surfaces from emerging into direct contact, they will be rapidly destroyed owing to a lack of sufficient thickness and strength to withstand the interfacial pressure. In consequence, the rubbing surfaces will direct contact again, bringing about serious friction and wear. As for the Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 dispersion, owing to the outermost layer of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N capped by negatively charged anions (N(CF3SO2)2‾), resulting in they can be preferentially absorbed onto the positively charged rubbing surfaces to form ordered physical absorption film by electrostatic attractions. The formation of physical absorption film could prevent the rubbing surfaces from arising direct contact to some extent, decreasing friction and wear. Additionally, the rigid carbon cores of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N on the rubbing surfaces can play the rolling, mending, and polishing effects to reduce the surface roughness and contact pressure, further alleviating friction and wear. As the tribo-test goes on, the Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N/PEG200 lubrication system gradually reacts with the metallic friction surfaces under harsh working conditions. By the complicated tribochemical reactions, the physical absorption films will be eventually replaced by the stronger tribo-film, which is mainly composed of metal oxides, metal carbonates, metal sulfates, metal sulfites, organic compounds (composed of C, O, N, F, and S), and Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N. What is particularly noteworthy is that the carbon cores of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N on the bottom of tribo-film could significantly promote its thickness and strength, protecting it from destruction. Therefore, the established tribo-film can hinder the rubbing surfaces that occurred in direct contact for a long time, thereby efficiently improving the friction-reduction, anti-wear, and load-supporting behaviors of the lubrication system even under harsh working conditions.

In summation, CDs-based additives in virtue of abundant anti-friction and anti-wear mechanisms, remarkable tribological performances, good designability, and eco-friendliness have become one of the most widely studied nano-additives. Numerous studies have shown that surface functionalization and carbon core doping are extremely effective approaches for further improving the tribological performance and application value of CDs-based additives. Therefore, the novel ionic liquids functionalized CDs-based additives (Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N) with doping by polymetallic atoms (Zn, Cu, and Fe atoms) concurrently were successfully prepared for the first time. The synthesized Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N as the lubricant additives could prominently promote the lubricating property of the PEG200 base oil. Specifically, when suitable concentrations of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N were introduced into the PEG base oil, which friction-reduction, anti-wear, and load-supporting functions can be maximally enhanced by 46.6 %, 66.3 %, and 125.0 %, respectively. Additionally, Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N also have excellent tribological behaviors even under harsh operating conditions, exhibiting good practical value. The prominent tribological performance of Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N is closely related to the ionic liquid groups and doped polymetallic atoms. The [AMIm][N(CF3SO2)2] groups and doped metal atoms would accelerate Zn,Cu,Fe-CDs-N to form strong lubricating films (physical absorption film and tribo-film) on the rubbing surfaces, then combine the nano-lubrication effects of rigid carbon cores to collectively reduce the friction and wear of the friction pairs. In short, this work has opened new avenues for exploiting high-performance CDs-based additives and provided reliable data support and theoretical guidance for designing and applying of the nano-lubricant additives.
